By Thomas G. Lester, P.E., Life Member ASHRAE
E
ngineers have relied on tools such as cardboard duct calculators,
 log-log graphs and lengthy tables for information that was difficult to cal-
culate directly. With the spreadsheets that are available today, calculations can
be made easily and placed into user-friendly functions. Building on the method
used in “Solving for Friction Factor,”1 this article presents user defined functions
(UDFs) that can be used as an alternative to the duct calculator.
The duct calculator typically is used
to find the duct diameter given flow
and design pressure drop. When the
cardboard duct calculator was created,
assumptions were made for the specific
volume, viscosity and absolute roughness
of the duct material. These assumptions
can lead to significant error, particularly
with the absolute roughness that varies
significantly between the duct materials
that commonly are used. The approach
in this article uses actual values for these
properties.
The duct calculator also can be used
to find the flow given the duct diameter
and design pressure drop or find the pres-
sure drop given flow and duct diameter.
It also provides a graphical solution for
rectangular equivalents to the diameter.
All of these functions are built into an
Microsoft Excel spreadsheet that can
be downloaded from www.ashrae.org/
ashraejournal/Lester\_Duct\_Calculator\_
UDFs.xls. This article provides a brief
explanation of how the spreadsheet and
UDFs were created, what user defined
functions are, and how they can be used.
There are many fine books on Office/Ex-
cel that provide a comprehensive review
of Visual Basic for Applications. You
will need to consult these references for
a complete understanding of the subject.
Calculations
Before writing the UDF procedure
for duct diameter, the calculations can
be done manually in a standard spread-
sheet. Here, it can more easily be tested,
debugged and verified for accuracy. For
someone not familiar with the creation
process for UDFs, it is also easier to see
what calculations are being executed.
The spreadsheet procedure, under the
“Dia Air” tab, is then replicated as a
UDF.
The procedure used to create these
functions is a form of iterative problem
solving. In some cases, a solution of the
Colebrook Equation is required. The
About the Author
Thomas G. Lester, P.E., is a retired senior me-
chanical engineer in Rochester, N.Y.
Using Excel for
Duct Calculations
User Defined Functions
This article was published in ASHRAE Journal, August 2009. Copyright 2009 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning
Engineers, Inc. Posted at www.ashrae.org. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission
of ASHRAE. For more information about ASHRAE Journal, visit www.ashrae.org.
August 2009
ASHRAE Journal
43
article, “Solving for Friction Factor,” was devel-
oped as an alternative solution for the Colebrook
Equation. In that article, a UDF named “fCalc”
was created to solve for the friction factor given
relative roughness and Reynolds number. This is
used to solve for friction factor in the new UDFs.
The same basic approach used in “fCalc” is used
as well.
While each case is unique, the general approach
follows five steps:
1. Determine the range over which the un-
known will be evaluated;
2. Perform a series of calculations where the
difference between the design value and the
actual value is calculated;
in Row 15. This tells us that a 2 in. (51 mm) diameter in
Row 14 has a pressure drop greater than the design drop,
but in Row 15, where the diameter is 3 in. (76 mm), the
opposite is true. We can conclude, therefore, that the true
value for the diameter lies between 2 in. and 3 in. (51 mm
and 76 mm).
Note that the plot of the difference (the blue line) crosses the
zero difference (the red line) between diameters of 2.00 and
3.00. The plot is a graphical solution of the problem; albeit
one that requires some refinement to accurately determine the
true value of diameter.
Step 4: Find an initial value for duct diameter, and repeat
Step 2 using a smaller increment. Excel has a built-in function,
“Vlookup.” This will return a value from a table based on a desir-
able condition. In this example, Column A has been duplicated
in Column K and a table was created in the range J14:K24. The
“Vlookup” function is looking for the value, or as the function
works, the last negative value in the first column of the table
(Column J) and returns the corresponding value in Column
K, in this case 2.00. With this information, a second series can
be created, starting with the value returned by the “Vlookup”
and incrementing by 1.05. In the same manner, a new table and
“Vlookup” will return a value that is within 5% of the true value.
(In this case, the second “Vlookup” returns a value of 2.10.)
Step 5: Perform a series of calculations, starting with the
value from Step 4, whereby an approximate slope is generated
at the value returned by the last “Vlookup.” The projected
diameter (ProjDia) is calculated using simple triangulation. (The
use of simple triangulation is made possible by knowing that
the value on the y axis is “0.”) Figure 2 shows this situation.
The blue line is the difference plotted against the diameter.
The red line is the zero difference line.
The green line (the slope) was generated by pairing the
last value from “Vlookup” multiplied by 0.999 with a second
The Excel workbook discussed here
is available online at www.ashrae.org/
ashraejournal/Lester\_Duct\_Calculator\_
UDFs.xls.
Figure 1: First table and plot in the spreadsheet.
3. Visualize a plot of the difference (from Step 2) versus
the unknown;
4. Find an initial value for the unknown and repeat Step 2
using a smaller increment; and
5. Perform a series of calculations, starting with the value
from Step 4, whereby an approximate slope of the plot is
calculated and then projected to the axis of the unknown.
Our objective is to find the duct diameter that satisfies the
condition of flow and design pressure drop given the properi-
ties of air (specific volume and viscosity) and duct material
(absolute roughness).
Step 1: Determine the range over which the unknown
will be evaluated. In the case of duct diameter, I chose a
minimum diameter of 2 in. (51 mm) and a maximum diameter
of approximately 115 in. (2933 mm). (Note that in the actual
UDF only the minimum diameter is important.)
Step 2: Perform a series of calculations, starting with the
minimum diameter where the difference between the design
pressure drop and the actual pressure drop is calculated.
Figure 1 shows a portion of the spreadsheet where this first set
of calculations takes place. The diameter in Column A is being
used in a series of trial and error calculation given fixed values
of flow, design pressure drop, specific volume, viscosity and
absolute roughness. Each row, starting in Row 15, increments
the diameter by a factor of 1.5. (A flow of 6 cfm [2.8 L/s] was
used because the graphic scales best with such a value.) The
actual pressure drop is calculated in Column H using the Darcy-
Weisbach equation, converted to inches of water. Column J
shows the difference between the design pressure drop and the
actual pressure drop.
Step 3: Visualize a plot of the difference versus the
unknown that is being solved for. The objective is to find
the diameter that is within 50% of the true value. Note that
the difference is negative in Row 14 but changes to positive
44
ASHRAE Journal
A u g u s t 2 0 0 9
Figure 2: In Step 5 the projected diameter is calculated using simple
triangulation.
Zero Difference
High Difference
Low
Difference
High
Diameter
Projected
Diameter
Low
Diameter
types of VBA macros: command macros (sometimes referred
as keystroke macros) and user defined functions (sometimes
referred to as function procedures). It is the user defined func-
tions that I will focus on.
point multiplied by 1.001. Figure 3 shows how this appears in
the spreadsheet. The first projected diameter is shown in Cell
M77. This value is then used in the same manner for the second
projection (shown in Cell M80). This is repeated for a third pro-
jection, resulting in a final solution, 2.1571 in. (55 mm) in Cell
M83. (Verification of this result is in the sample spreadsheet.)
As crude as this procedure may seem, it produces precise
results in three projections.
Notice the difference in Cell J83 is negative and in Cell J84
it is positive. We have reached the limits in accuracy for the
increments used here (meaning the 0.999 and 1.001 multipliers).
If greater accuracy is desired, finer increments must be used.
When writing this procedure as a user defined function, I’ve
used a “Do Loop Until,” ending the loop when I reach a nega-
tive difference for the first of the pair and a positive difference
for the second line of the pair.
This spreadsheet was presented to show the calculation but is
good for only one point at a time. The calculation has been written
into a UDF named “AirDia,” which makes the procedure easy to
use in a network of ducts with multiple points. The next section
gives a brief explanation of UDFs, and how they can be used.
Visual Basic for Applications (VBA) and Excel
Visual Basic for Applications (VBA) is included in Excel.
Among the uses of VBA is the creation of macros. There are two
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
August 2009
ASHRAE Journal
45
Access to VBA has changed in Excel 2007. Under the “Of-
fice Button,” click on “Excel Options.” Under the “Popular
Tab,” “Top Options for Working with Excel,” check the
“Show Developer Tab in the Ribbon.” Select the “Devel-
oper” tab and Visual Basic will appear in the left side of
the options. In Excel 2003 and older versions, select Tools
> Macros >Visual Basic Editor. Either path takes you to the
Visual Basic Editor. UDFs are created and/or edited in the
Modules section.
Viewing a User Defined Function
You do not need to view a UDF to use it. This section is provided
for anyone wishing to look at the code used in creating the UDFs.
While UDFs follow the general format of Visual Basic pro-
gramming, they don’t necessarily contain any special program-
ming language. The sample spreadsheet includes five UDFs;
the simplest is the procedure for calculating the pressure drop
in an airstream, “AirPD,” shown below:
Function AirPD(CFM, Dia, AbsRough, KineVisc, SpVol)
Dim Vel As Single
Dim RelRough As Single
Dim ReynoldsNum As Single
Dim FF As Single
Dim Darcy As Single
Vel = CFM / (3.14159 \* (Dia / 12) ^ 2 / 4)
RelRough = AbsRough \* 12 / Dia
ReynoldsNum = Vel / 60 \* (Dia / 12) / KineVisc
FF = fCalc(RelRough, ReynoldsNum)
Darcy = (Vel / 60) ^ 2 / (2 \* 32.174) \* FF \* 100 \* 12 / Dia /
(62.4 \* SpVol) \* 12
AirPD = Darcy
End Function
The first line uses the “Function” keyword, identifying the
procedure as a UDF. “AirPD” is the function name, followed
by the “arguments” inside parentheses. These “arguments” are
values to be used in the calculations.
The “Dim Statements” are then used to establish variables
and the precision to be used. The “As Single” will apply a
single-precision floating point to a variable.
Calculation statements for velocity, relative roughness and
Reynolds number follow. The calculation of friction factor (FF)
uses the procedure fCalc (Module11) that was introduced in
“Solving for Friction Factor.” The last calculation is the Darcy-
Weisbach formula with conversions to units of inches of water.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
46
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
A u g u s t 2 0 0 9
The statement “AirPD = Darcy” sets
the name of the UDF equal to the result-
ing calculation. The “End Function” key-
words indicate the end of the procedure.
In this case, all the statements are simple
mathematical computations. In fact, I use
only one Visual Basic procedure in any of
the UDFs presented: a “Do Loop Until.” I
use this to replicate the tables and also to
execute the slope and projection calcula-
tions. The calculations are identical to the spreadsheet. In the
tables, I stop the incrementing as soon as a positive value is calcu-
lated for the design pressure drop minus the actual pressure drop.
Using a Recorded UDF
There are five UDFs in the sample spreadsheet.
1. fCalc (RelRough, ReynoldsNum) (Module11). This UDF
solves for friction factor given relative roughness and
Reynolds number. Note: fCalc must be imported to make
the other functions work properly because it is embedded
in the other functions.
2. AirDia (CFM, DesignStatic, AbsRough, KineVisc,
SpVol) (Module2). This UDF solves for duct diameter
given the arguments cfm, design static, absolute rough-
ness, kinematic viscosity, and specific volume.
3. AirCap (Dia, DesignStatic, AbsRough, KineVisc, SpVol)
(Module1). This UDF solves for the flow (CFM) given
the stated arguments.
4. AirPD (CFM, Dia, AbsRough, KineVisc, SpVol) (Module5).
This UDF solves for pressure drop given the stated arguments.
5. WCalc (Height, EquivDia) (Module3). This UDF solves
for the Huebscher equivalents for rectangular duct.2
When opening a spreadsheet that contains macros, Excel asks
to “Enable Macros.” For the UDFs to execute, you must “Enable
Macros.” In Excel 2003 and older versions, a dialog box appears
when opening a spreadsheet with macros. This is less obvious in
Excel 2007, where only a “Security Warning” appears below the
“ribbon” and an “Options” button appears. Click on “Options”
and select, “Enable this content.” Without “Enable Macros,” the
spreadsheet will not perform any of the calculations described.
UDFs work the same as the hundreds of built-in functions exist-
ing in Excel. Available functions are listed by categories. UDFs are
placed in a category named “User Defined,” which is automatically
created whenever UDFs are created. In this category, all the UDFs
in “Add-ins,” as well as those created in the spreadsheet, are shown.
If a new spreadsheet is created, the sample spreadsheet can
be used by deleting all of the worksheets that are not desired.
When this is saved, all of the UDFs will be saved with it.
The sample spreadsheet contains examples of the use of each
of these functions. Under the “Examples” tab, the procedure to
enter a UDF would be:
1. Place the cursor in Cell I22 (or any blank cell).
2. In Excel 2007, select the “Formulas” tab and then select
“Insert Function” or from Excel 2003 or older, just click
on the “fx” icon.
Figure 3: Slope and projection triangles.
3. The function dialog box appears, and then select “User
Defined” from the categories dropdown menu.
4. From the list of available functions, select “AirDia.”
5. Another dialog box opens with the “arguments” listed.
Enter relative references for flow and absolute rough-
ness and absolute references (using the F4 key after the
cell reference is entered) for the global inputs for design
static, kinematic viscosity and specific volume. (Global
references will apply to all duct sections.)
6. Once the “arguments” have been added, the results should
appear in the dialog box; click “ok” and the UDF will be
completed in Cell I22.
7. The UDF can be copied to the range I23:I26.
8. If the “arguments” have been entered correctly, I22:I26
should match D22:D26.
For use in an existing spreadsheet, an Excel add-in can be
created by saving the sample spreadsheet in .xla format. This
is accomplished by performing a “Save As” and specifying
“.xla” format. The saved add-in can be used as any other
Excel add-in.
Alternatively, the modules could be exported from the sample
spreadsheet and imported into an existing spreadsheet. While
this requires some familiarity with the Visual Basic Editor, it
is relatively easy to export and import the modules. Within
the editor, under the “File” menu, you will see the commands
“Export File” and “Import File.” Highlight a module and then
export it as a “.bas” file. The module can be imported into an
existing spreadsheet. When all the modules have been added,
save the spreadsheet and close it. Re-open the spreadsheet, “En-
able Macros,” and they will appear under the “User Defined”
category in the functions dialog box.
Conclusions
Engineering tools such as the cardboard duct calculator per-
form functions that easily can be performed directly in a spread-
sheet using user defined functions. By using the actual values
for properties of air and duct material, it is possible to improve
the accuracy of the results. With practice and familiarity, these
calculations can be performed more efficiently using UDFs.
References
1. Lester, T. 2003. “Solving for Friction Factor.” ASHRAE Journal
45(7):41 – 44.
2.  2005 ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals, Chapter 35, Duct
Design, p. 35.9.

By Kishor Khankari, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
About the Author
Kishor Khankari, Ph.D., is associate partner at
Syska Hennessy Group in Ann Arbor, Mich. He
is a member of ASHRAE Technical Committee
(TC) 9.9, Mission Critical Facilities, Technology
Spaces and Electronic Equipment, and TC 9.11,
Clean Spaces.
Rack Enclosures
A Crucial Link in Airflow Management in Data Centers
R
ack enclosures that host servers are not only cabinets; they are an integral
part of the airflow system of data centers. The cold air supplied from the
air-conditioning units must freely pass through rack enclosures without any
obstructions. If the rack enclosures excessively restrict airflow, it can adversely
affect the cooling performance of the servers and disturb the overall balance
of the entire airflow system.
Air is the main carrier of heat and moisture in the data center. Proper man-
agement of cold and hot airstreams in the data center is crucial for maintaining
acceptable levels of inlet air temperatures to the servers as recommended in
the recent ASHRAE thermal guidelines.1 However, following only best practices
for data center design, without properly considering rack enclosure design, is
not sufficient to obtain desired cooling performance of a data center.
Interactions with Server Fans
Servers are equipped with their own
fans to move the air through the server
chassis. Design airflow rates determined
through bench tests of servers generally
cannot anticipate and account for all the
obstructions surrounding the servers
when placed in the racks. Thermal tests
conducted on a fully loaded 42U rack
showed as much as 9°F (5°C) increase
in the server inlet air temperatures due
to surrounding obstructions.2,3 These ob-
structions in the path of air reduce the air-
flow rates through the server fans, which
can reduce the cooling and increase the
This article was published in ASHRAE Journal, August 2009. Copyright 2009 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning
Engineers, Inc. Posted at www.ashrae.org. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission
of ASHRAE. For more information about ASHRAE Journal, visit www.ashrae.org.
August 2009
ASHRAE Journal
49
exit air temperature from the servers. The extent of
such a reduction in the fan performance depends
on the individual design of the racks.
Figure 1 illustrates the hypothetical interaction
between the server fan and the system resistance
offered by the racks. The intersection between
the system performance curve and the server
fan performance curve determines the operat-
ing airflow rate for the server rack system. The
dotted curve shown in this figure represents the
system curve for a single server during the bench
test analysis. This figure further indicates, due
to increased resistance to airflow, that the oper-
ating airflows through the server rack systems
can be lower than the desired airflow rate of the
bench test of a single server. As the restrictions
in the rack enclosures increase, the operating
airflow through the servers can decrease. More
restrictive rack enclosure designs can reduce
the operating airflow rates through the servers.
Rack Enclosure Designs Play Important Role
As mentioned previously, the design of a rack
enclosure plays an important role in determining
the airflow rate through the servers. Some of the
with ceiling plenum return (closed loop) system, all the fans
(including fans of air-handling units, server fans, and any ad-
ditional fans in the racks) operate in series, fans in the cabinets
can become a bottleneck in delivering desired airflow from
the air-handling units.
Rack heat loads and how the loads are distributed within the
racks can be another factor that can affect airflow through the
rack enclosures. Several combinations of these design concepts
are available in the market. Consider all of these factors and
evaluate their effect on server fan performance before selecting
rack enclosures for data centers.
Pressure Buildup and Air Recirculation
Pressure can build up in the gap between the back perforated
door and back of servers from where hot air exits from the
servers. Such a pressure buildup can cause internal recircula-
tion of hot air within the cabinet that can be entrained into the
servers. Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) analysis was
performed for a typical rack enclosure with a front-to-back
airflow path to illustrate how such a pressure buildup can af-
fect airflow within the racks and the cooling performance of
servers. This model considered a 42U rack with 63% open
area ratio of front and back perforated doors. The heat load
in the model varied from 5 kW to 20 kW. Accordingly, the
airflow rates through servers and the total supply airflow rates
were proportionately adjusted to maintain a 20°F (11°C) rise
(DT) across the servers.
CFD analysis predicts the pressure distribution, temperature
distribution, and airflow patterns within the rack enclosure.
As shown in Figure 2, CFD analysis predicts higher pressure
Server Fan Curve
Rack Design 2
Rack Design 1
Bench Test
Delivered Airflow Rates
Design
Airflow
Rate
Pressure Drop
Airflow Rate
factors that can affect the performance of server fans due to
the design of racks are as follows. A typical rack enclosure
consists of front and back perforated doors, railings to hold the
servers, and several cables that occupy the space between the
back perforated door or blind panel. All of these components
create obstructions to the airflow through the racks.
In addition, the airflow paths within rack enclosures are
crucial. Most racks are designed for a front entry and back
exit flow path for the cooling air. Other variations of the
flow paths are front entry and top exit or bottom entry and
top exit. As air takes more turns before exiting the rack, it
induces pressure loss in the system for which a server fan
must compensate.
A few manufacturers offer a total containment of hot air
within the rack enclosures by using ducted exhaust. In these
racks the hot air is exhausted from the top either into the room or
into a ceiling return. This type of system avoids short-circuiting
and mixing of hot air with the room air. Since the air must pass
through narrow spaces, the server fans are required to work
harder to overcome these additional resistances. If this type
of system is not designed properly, it can result in reduced fan
performance.
Another design offers a rear door heat exchanger to provide
instant cooling of exiting hot air. The heat exchanger can create
additional resistance to the airflow, which can deteriorate the
server fan performance. Other designs offer additional fans
in the cabinets to assist in exhausting the hot air. All fans in
the data center, including the fans of the air-handling units,
should work in harmony with other fans to deliver desired
airflow rates and cooling performance. In the ducted exhaust
Figure 1: The intersection between the server fan curve and the system curve
determines the operating airflow rates through the system. This illustration shows
operating airflow rates through the server rack system are lower than the desired
airflow rates determined for a single server during the bench test. More restrictive
rack enclosure designs allow less airflow through the system.
50
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
A u g u s t 2 0 0 9
in the gap between the back perforated door and the server.
Such a pressure buildup as shown in Figure 3 drives the hot
air from the back to the front of the servers where it is picked
up again by the server fans. This recirculation of hot air results
in the higher inlet air temperatures than the desired supply
air temperature.
Figure 4 shows the distribution of inlet air temperatures
at server inlets at various levels in the rack. It shows a rim
of higher temperatures at both edges of servers. Higher inlet
air temperatures at the bottom servers are due to additional
entrainment of hot air from the back and under the cabinets.
Castors raise the level of the cabinet and create a gap between
the floor and the cabinet bottom. Hot air from the back finds

can build up in the gap between the back perforated door and the serv-

higher air recirculation with rack enclosures with increasing heat

recirculation can further increase the pressure buildup, which can
deteriorate the server fan performance.

ASHRAE Journal
51
www.info.hotims.com/ 25207-17
its way through this gap and enters into the bottom servers
from the front.
CFD analysis was performed with a 20% excess of cold sup-
ply air than what is required for a 20°F (11°C) rise through the
servers. In spite of such an excess supply, the predicted inlet
air temperatures are higher than the supply air temperature and
exceeded ASHRAE recommended higher temperature limit of
80.6°F (27°C).1 This is due to the recirculation of air within the
rack and entrainment of hot air under the rack.
Rack Heat Load Matters
As shown in Figures 5a and 5b, air recirculation becomes
worse with increasing heat loads in the rack. With increase in
the rack heat loads from 5 kW to 20 kW and proportionate
increase in the airflow rates through the servers, the pressure
difference between the back and front of the rack increases by
21%. As a result the extent of air recirculation into the server
intakes increases from 20% to 45%. Recirculation within the
rack is avoided by placing additional plates to block the flow
of air from the back of the servers. However, Figure 5b shows
such a blocking can further increase the pressure buildup that,
in turn, can adversely affect the server fan performance and
reduce the airflow rate through the server. The design of the
back plenum is crucial to avoid such a pressure buildup.
To achieve adequate cooling, the speed of a server fan increas-
es with the increase in the temperature of electronic components
and with a rise in the inlet air temperature. However, such a
ramp up in the fan speeds can increase energy consumption
(fan energy is proportional to the cube of the fan speed). The
dc fan power of servers can increase 3.5 to 7 times when server
inlet air temperature increases from around 80°F to 95°F (44°C
to 53°C).4 In addition, increased airflow rates, such as at ramp
up, can further promote pressure buildup behind the servers,
which can affect fan performance.
Summary
Rack enclosures are a crucial link between data center air-
handling units and servers. Design of rack enclosures can impact
the fan performance, and therefore, cooling performance of
servers. Careful consideration should be given before selecting
the rack enclosures for a certain data center. Just following the
best practices for the design and layout of data centers is not
enough to obtain the optimum cooling performance. This article
illustrates the importance of rack enclosure design and airflow
management within the rack enclosures.
References
1. ASHRAE. 2008. “2008 ASHRAE Environmental Guidelines for
Datacom Equipment—Expanding the Recommended Environmental
Envelope.”
2. Artman, P., D. Moss, G. Bennett. 2002. “Dell™ Power-
Edge™1650: Rack Impacts on Cooling for High Density Servers.”
Dell White Paper. Enterprise Systems Group (ESG).
3. Moss, D. 2009. Personal communications.
4. Jones, R., et al. 2008. “Seven Strategies to Improve Data Center
Cooling Efficiency.” The Green Grid White Paper 11.

By Stephane Sirard, P.E., Member ASHRAE; and Chantale Bourdages, P.E.
W
 hen people think of Montreal, they think of snow, cold winds, winter
carnivals and ice sculptures. However, when one looks on a map,
Montreal is only on the 45th northern latitude. If one were to follow the 45th
latitude across the Atlantic Ocean, the city of Lyon, France, could be considered
Montreal’s European equivalent, latitude-wise.
However, Montreal winters have nothing in common with Lyon, or any other
city in France for that matter. With its 4,575 heating degree-days, Montreal is
closer cousins with Moscow, even if Moscow is on the 55th northern latitude
(Figure 1). Even more impressive is that Montreal’s average minimum monthly
winter temperatures are comparable to Anchorage, Alaska (Figure 2).
If Eastern Canada is shockingly cold,
it’s because of an oceanic current called
the Labrador Current. The current flows
from the Arctic Ocean south along the
Atlantic coast.
The legendary association between Que-
bec and cold winters is so strong that many
people forget that Montreal also has hot
and humid summers. Montreal summers
are actually similar to Lyon or New York
summers. Normal temperature variations
in the Montreal region are around 40°C
(72°F), as shown in Figure 2. Sporadically,
temperatures can drop to –34°C (–30°F) or
rise to 34°C (93°F). These extreme varia-
About the Authors
Stephane Sirard, P.E., is chief engineer and HVAC
designer and Chantale Bourdages, P.E., is HVAC
designer at Dessau in Longueuil, QC, Canada.
Geothermal for
Grain Research Center
This article was published in ASHRAE Journal, August 2009. Copyright 2009 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning
Engineers, Inc. Posted at www.ashrae.org. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission
of ASHRAE. For more information about ASHRAE Journal, visit www.ashrae.org.
August 2009
ASHRAE Journal
53
tions make building design in the Montreal region complicated,
especially when designing energy-efficient buildings.
When the CÉROM, a public interest agricultural research
center located a few miles south of Montreal (St-Mathieu-de-
Beloeil), decided to build a research center that would satisfy
sustainable development while achieving high energy efficiency,
the design team knew they had a challenge. In addition to the
25,600 ft2 (2378 m2) main building, the CÉROM planned on
building three 1,700 ft2 (158 m2) controlled greenhouses dedi-
cated to research. Greenhouse energy costs during the winter
are high in Quebec, adding on to the project’s complexity.
Energy Simulation Software
Many different solutions were studied to determine what type
of HVAC systems would be most efficient and fit in the strict
budget. The design team decided to use
DOE2.1e simulation software to help
analyze the different possibilities. En-
ergy simulation software programs such
as DOE, are powerful tools that can cal-
culate parameters that have concomitant
effects on a building’s energy consump-
tion. They simultaneously account for
weather parameters, building envelope
parameters and complex HVAC system
interactions.
Detailed models of the CÉROM’s
main building and greenhouses were
made. With these models the design
team could foresee the building’s en-
ergy consumption when equipped with
different types of systems. First, a refer-
ence building was created as a basis for
comparison. The reference building was
a model of the CÉROM research center
with standard HVAC systems following
the Canadian Model National Energy Code for Buildings.
Through energy simulation models the design team was able
to analyze the CÉROM building and greenhouses and fully un-
derstand their yearly behaviors and needs. The designers could
predict long-term costs and evaluate the actual cost of efficient
equipment that appeared more expensive at first glance.
Thanks to the use of building energy simulation software the
designers were able to prove that clean and quiet renewable en-
ergy was worth the initial investment. Energy recovery systems
were also used to create an integrated system that generates
important energy and financial savings. Table 1 shows the differ-
ent energy efficiency strategies that were used for the CÉROM.
Heating and Cooling
Heat pumps are known to consume at least three times less
energy than conventional HVAC systems. Their efficiency is
further increased when they are connected to a ground heat
exchanger. Renewable ground source energy is ideal for ex-
treme climate regions like Quebec (Figures 1 and 2). During
winter the geothermal loops collect heat stored in the ground
to heat the building. During summer the geothermal loops
reject excess heat to cool the building, slowly recharging the
ground exchanger for the upcoming winter. The geothermal
heat exchanger has an alternating cycle that works because
of Quebec’s extreme seasons. If only heating or cooling were
needed year-round, the ground could not be recharged, and it
would be drained of its heating or cooling properties.
A total of 24 vertical geothermal loops were installed at a
depth of 500 ft (152 m) at the CÉROM grain research center. The
geothermal loops are paired with 29 water-to-air heat pumps.
Generally, greenhouses using geothermal energy choose direct
expansion systems with horizontal geothermal loops. However,
the designers chose to use an indirect vertical exchanger, creat-
ing a system that could be used for the greenhouses and the
main building.
Fresh Air
The makeup air system solves the
problem of preheating the outdoor fresh
air entering the building. Since Montre-
al winters are so cold (Figures 1 and 2),
it takes a lot of energy to bring outdoor
air to a comfortable 22°C (72°F). With
proper control sequences, heat recovery
and a solar wall considerably reduce the
amount of energy required.
First, the fresh air goes through the
solar wall. Solar walls are made of a
highly absorbent metallic panel with
small holes allowing the intake of
fresh air (Figure 3). Facing southwest,
the 688 ft2 (64 m2) solar wall at the
CÉROM preheats outside fresh air and
recovers the building envelope heat loss
by transferring it back to the incoming
fresh air. Surprisingly, the solar wall does not increase cooling
needs during the summer. During warm periods another fresh
air intake is used, and the solar wall helps the southbound wall
stay cool by ventilating the wall.
After being preheated by the solar wall, the fresh air goes
through a heat exchanger called an enthalpy wheel that recovers
heat from the exhaust air. Enthalpy wheels are rotary air-to-
air heat exchangers that transfer sensible and latent heat. The
efficiency of the enthalpy wheel installed at the CÉROM is
evaluated at 75%. The combination of the enthalpy wheel and
solar wall considerably increases the outdoor air temperature,
reducing the effort required by the heating system. The remain-
ing fresh air heating demand is supplied by a geothermal heat
pump, providing good performance.
Refrigeration
The CÉROM building has different types of controlled envi-
ronments that are used for research. In addition to greenhouses,
the building has controlled atmosphere rooms (CONVIRON),
Greenhouse energy consumption is usually high.

loop size. The greenhouses required a separate evaluation
because of the important heat loss through their envelopes.
Although, greenhouses represent a small percentage of the
total building area, their annual energy consumption is high.
When adding the cost equation to the part-load efficiency
optimization, the designers concluded that the ideal percent-
age of the maximum capacity that should be assured by the
geothermal system stood at 56% for the greenhouses and 78%
for the main building.
Figure 5 shows how when the heat pumps are selected to
meet 56% of the greenhouse peak loads; energy consumption
barely increases when compared to a 100% heat pump system
(blue curve). This is explained by the fact that at 56% of peak
loads the heat pumps meet heating needs over 85% of the time
(red curve). In the main building, electrical coils were installed
to meet the residual heating needs. However, since greenhouse
production quality is sensitive to power failures and since
natural gas was not available in that specific area, the design

cold rooms and freezers. These areas have spe-
cific condensers to control the temperature. The
heat rejected from the condensers is injected into
the geothermal loop, providing additional free
heating.
Intelligent Control System
One of the easiest ways to reduce energy con-
sumption is to turn off equipment when it is not
being used. This was taken into account when
designing the control components for the HVAC
systems to manage energy consumption. The
HVAC systems are programmed to be turned off
for any unoccupied hour.
Centralized control systems were installed to
increase the maintenance and operations readi-
ness. Since the main building and the greenhouses
have different building needs, they each have their
own control system that tells the maintenance
personnel when operations should be carried out
(e.g., filter replacement or lubrication of equip-
ment parts).
Cost Effectiveness
With Montreal’s extreme temperature varia-
tions, heating and cooling maximum peak
loads are high. Not only must one choose elec-
tromechanical equipment that meet maximum
peak loads, but one must also ensure that this
equipment will provide adequate efficiency
when systems meet partial loads. The average
heating or cooling partial load for a building in
the Montreal region is generally under 50% of
the peak load. It is important to choose equip-
ment that can offer high efficiency throughout
the year. Energy simulation models are essential
tools to evaluate building part-load ratios and
system behaviors.
In this case, the designers opted for a geo-
thermal system with heat pumps. However, as
excavation and geothermal materials are quite
costly, in addition, heat pump efficiency is
reduced considerably when used at part load,
simulations were made with the DOE2.1e and
GS2000 software applications to evaluate the
optimal heat pump’s capacity and geothermal

Figure 3: Solar wall preheats outside fresh air.
team chose an oil boiler to handle residual heating needs in the
greenhouses. The boiler only works for about 15% of the yearly
number of heating hours. Thanks to this optimal combination
of systems, it was possible to reduce the size of the geothermal
exchanger to only 56% of the greenhouse peak loads, keeping
costs to a minimum. By reducing the size of the heat pumps
the design ensures that the equipment will work more hours at
full capacity, maximizing heat pump efficiency.
Risks
The use of new equipment is always risky. Nonstandard
equipment is more costly and usually means more time and care
during the design phase. This inevitably increases a project’s
complexity. However, thanks to energy simulation software,
the design team was able to ensure the economic advantages of
the innovative concept, reducing the risks of nonconventional
installations.

Energy Efficiency and Financial Impact
The choice of systems and efficient
equipment allowed a yearly reduction
of 2.3 billion Btu (673 728 kWh). The
CÉROM’s building design uses 54% less
energy than an equivalent building with
standard HVAC systems respecting the
Canadian Model National Energy Code
for Buildings. DOE2.1e building simula-
tion software was used to perform these
calculations (Figure 6).
The CÉROM budget was estab-
lished at $5.2 million. The overrun
suppliers. The payback period is evalu-
ated at 4.3 years.
Environmental Impact
The building’s particular concept
significantly reduces energy consump-
tion (by more than 54%) by primar-
ily using renewable energy. This also
reduces greenhouse gas emissions by
347 tons (315 Mg) a year.
The use of CFC and HCFC refrig-
erants often used in heat pumps, are
known to contribute to global warm-
ing. The heat pumps at CÉROM’s
building use only HFC refrigerants
(R-410 and SP22). By selecting an
indirect geothermal system, the prob-
ability of ground contamination is
greatly reduced compared to a tra-
ditional geothermal direct expansion
system that circulates refrigerant
directly into the ground loop.
As a whole, the CÉROM building is
a good example of sustainable develop-
ment that easily could be used for future
buildings.

is that each component takes time
to respond, thus contributing to the

making it necessary to include time as
a variable in the system analysis.
Transients

analysis implies that conditions are

Fundamentals of
Linear Control Theory
In the following presentation of linear feedback control theory, the author discusses basic concepts, time
constants, system analysis, and stability.
teristics. Take, for example, the room
temperature control system shown
in Fig. 1. Changes in the load affect
the room air temperature (output) in
a manner determined by the room
characteristics (controlled system).
The temperature change is sensed by
the bimetal (sensing element). The
resulting motion activates the valve
top (control elements), resulting in a
change in valve position. This change
BASIC CONCEPTS
There are three topics to consider
under basic concepts: (1) systems, (2)
transients, and (3) feedback.
Systems
It is necessary to consider the
entire control system as an entity.
Every element has an influence on
the output and must be considered in
determining the overall system charac-
WILLIAM P. CHAPMAN, P.E., PRESIDENTIAL MEMBER/FELLOW ASHRAE
William P. Chapman, P.E., Presidential Member/Fellow
ASHRAE, served as president of ASHRAE in 1976 – 77 and was
the director of research and development at Johnson Service
Company, Milwaukee.
After joining ASHVE in 1947, Chapman served
on many Society committees. His service included
the Presidential Ad Hoc International Standards
Committee, the Research Administrative Council,
a task group on air-conditioning system control,
a task group on dynamic response, the Research
and Technical Committee, chairing the Joint Expo Policy
Committee, the Scholarship Trust Fund, the Global Warming
Position Paper Committee, the Nominating Committee, the
Publications Committee and the Guide Book Committee.
He served on the Historical Committee and TC 6.1, Hydronic
and Steam Equipment Systems. He was elevated to the grade
of Fellow in 1969 and received the Distinguished Service Award
in 1969, the F. Paul Anderson Award in 1983, the ASHRAE-ALCO
Medal for Distinguished Public Service in 1993, and the
Distinguished 50-Year Member Award in 1998.
When presenting Chapman with the Anderson
award, Presidential Member Clinton Phillips recog-
nized him as “an engineer, researcher and author who
has shaped the technical evolution of building system
controls, benefiting mankind through the improvement of
living conditions.”
Chapman retired from Johnson Controls in 1984. He died in
April 2002 at age 82.

The system shown in Fig. 1 is a
closed loop system. When a system
does not have feedback, it is an open
loop system. In open loops the cor-
rective effort is determined by the
set point alone. Room temperature
controlled by an outside tempera-
ture controller is an example of an
open loop system. In general, better
control is obtained by a closed loop
system.
So far we have considered only
system feedback; however, there
is also a secondary feedback when
the controller itself has a feedback
loop, this is frequently referred to
as internal feedback. The benefit of
feedback within an instrument lies
in the ability to use larger signals and
then reduce the signal by feedback.
This generally develops superior per-
formance without need for precision
manufacturing.
TIME CONSTANTS
There are two topics that should
be considered under the concept of
time constants: (1) response time (or
lag) and (2) dead time (or transporta-
tion lag). When we discussed systems
it was indicated that each component
takes time to respond to a change
in input. This response time, T, is
related to the physical properties
of the component; therefore, it is
predictable and independent of the
change in input.
The two properties necessary to
establish T are resistance, R, and
capacitance, C. These are usually
thought of as electrical terms, but
actually can be thermal or hydraulic
terms also. As in electrical terminol-
ogy, resistance is the opposition to
flow, and capacitance is the change
in quantity per unit change in
potential.
Potential can be given in units of
electrical strength, temperature, or
pressure. The product of resistance
and capacitance determines the
response time, written T = RC. The
units of T are time units (seconds,
minutes, etc.).
If a system has only one resistance
and one capacitance, it is called a
first-order system, and the value of
T explicitly determines the time
response of the system. If, however,
there are two or more resistances and
ca­pacitances in a system, there is no
single time constant as such, and the
response is related to the products of
the individual resistances and capaci-
changing with time. This consider-
ation is fundamental to the under-
standing of control theory. In the
above example, the load is influenced
by atmospheric conditions such as
wind, temperature, solar radiation,
etc. Because these factors change
continuously, the system is always in
a transient state; thus, the transient
behavior is fundamental.
A quantity which is solely depen-
dent on time is called a function of
time and is mathematically written
as f(t) or g(t). In this article, we will
deal with such functions in systems of
linear devices. A device is defined as
linear if its output to input ratio is a
function of time and not dependent
on the magnitude of the input signal.
For example, if the output of a device
is f(t) for an input of g(t), then the
output will be 2f(t) for an input of
2g(t).
A system is linear if all the devices
in that system are linear. Strictly
speaking, if hysteresis, saturation, or
dead zone is present, the system is not
linear. If the effects of these non-lin-
earities are small, however, or if they
fall outside the range of operation, the
general behavior can be described by
linear analysis.
Feedback
The third basic concept is feed-
back, and we will see how the system
concept and the transient concept are
brought together with the concept
of feedback. The significance of feed-
back is that it permits the continuous
adjustment of the system so as to
minimize the difference between the
actual output and a desired output
(set point). When a system has feed-
back, it is called a closed loop system.
  FIG. 1
Block diagram of a typical closed loop control system.

may be truly unstable. Most systems encountered in the
field are much higher than third order but, since many
of their characteristics can be neglected, it is possible to
approximate the response of the system by using an equiva-
lent third or even second-order system for the purpose of
analysis.
SYSTEM ANALYSIS
Armed with these concepts and tools, we are now ready
to consider system analysis. The acceptability of a con­trol
system usually can be described by two interdependent
criteria: (1) steady state and (2) dynamic per­formance.
Generally, one must be compromised somewhat by the
other. We will now try to see how these two criteria are
related, and also to see their relative importance in describ-
ing the complete system.
Steady-State Criteria
As indicated previously, an actual system never truly
reaches equilibrium, however, for all practical purposes sys-
tems do settle out so that the output falls within the offset
band as shown in Fig. 3. The width of this band is specified
and is a yardstick with which to measure control accuracy.
One way to reduce offset is to increase controller gain,
another is to use reset (more properly called integral
action). An increase in gain increases the chances of insta-
bility and the introduction of reset action decreases the
speed of response, in other words, each has its disadvantage
and the improvement of the steady-state performance is
made at the expense of dynamic performance.
tances. The number of these products that exist determines
the order of the system.
Let us consider an example of a stem thermometer in
thermal equilibrium suddenly being immersed in a bath
at a higher temperature. The thermal resistance retards
the heat flow from the bath into the thermometer and the
capacitance (mass and specific heat) determines the rate of
temperature rise. As the thermometer warms, the rate of
heat flow diminishes, due to the reduced thermal potential,
and finally reaches zero when the bath and thermometer
are at the same temperature. At any instant the thermom-
eter temperature is E = I (l – e –t/T) , where I is the initial
temperature difference and t is the time after immersion.
If T is known, the entire time response curve can be com-
puted for this first-order system. A typical curve for a first-
order system is shown in Fig. 2.
However, if the thermometer were placed in a well, then
the resistance and capacitance of the well would enter into
the response curve. If it is assumed that Tl represents the
response time of the thermometer and T2 the well, then
the above example would be E = I (I – Ae –t/T1 + Be –t/T2).
(Note: A and B are constants determined by Tl and T2.)
This is a second-order system, and no single value such as
T suffices to completely explain the system response.
Another complication arises if the thermometer signal is
transmitted sufficiently far to cause a measurable delay. For
example, assume that the thermometer is replaced by an
instrument called a temperature transmitter, and that this
transmitter is placed some 500 ft away from the receiver.
The 500 ft will be connected in this example by a copper
tube that will transmit a pneumatic signal. The signal (in
this case a pressure) will now be restricted in the tube and
the capacitance of the receiver will add a time constant
term to the response equation (remember the transmitter
will also have a response time,).
In addition to these two response times, the signal will take
a certain amount of time to travel the 500 ft. This lag is called
transportation lag, or dead time. This delay is not serious in
an indicating system, but can be significant and worth con-
sidering in a controlling system. Fig. 2 illustrates three typical
response curves for the examples that have been discussed.
It can be shown mathematically that a single-order
system cannot overshoot; hence, it is always stable. A
second-order system may oscillate but is always stable from
a mathematical standpoint. A third or higher order system

Dynamic Criteria
There are two criteria for system performance in the
dynamic state. (1) transient response and (2) frequency
response. Transient response refers to the response of a sys-
tem to a single, or non-recurring, change of input, as for
example, a step change. The output signal is recorded as a
function of time. The equation of this time­output plot is
used to determine a transfer function. This mathematical
expression enables the engineer to predict and describe the
complete system performance.
Frequency response refers to the response of a system
to a sinusoidally changing input. Again, the output is
recorded as a function of time. From this plot, a ratio of
output to input amplitude is made and the phase shift of
output and input sinusoids is determined. These terms are
then used to derive the transfer function of the system.
In air-conditioning systems, response times are quite long
and, as a result, frequency response techniques are seldom
feasible on an actual system.
Use of Transient Response
Transient response data can be expressed by two
indexes—overshoot and settling time. The proper combi-
nation of these determines the acceptability of the dynamic
performance, just as offset determines the acceptability
of the steady-state performance. Improvements in these
indexes can be made by adjusting the controller gain or by
using additional modes, such as rate action, and/or reset
action.
The purpose of system analysis is to enable the designer
or operator to adjust the system so as to achieve the new
steady state in as short a time as is consistent with the limi-
tations of the equipment and in such a way as to maintain
specified steady-state performance.
STABILITY
The fourth and last part of this paper deals with the
most important characteristic of a control system­—sta-
bility. Typical response curves as given in Fig. 2 were
indicative of stable systems. Fig. 4, however, illustrates an
unstable system. The significant difference in these two
illustrations can be described in terms of the way the final
value is reached after an input disturbance.
Additionally, however, we have a system, as illustrated
in Fig. 3, that for practical purposes can also be considered
a stable system and that reaches a final value in still a third
manner. Let us consider these three classes of systems. They
are overdamped, underdamped, and unstable. Overdamped
systems, as illustrated in Fig. 2, gradually approach the
final value, and at no time have an output signal that
exceeds the desired final value. Underdamped systems,
sometimes called oscillatory systems, are illustrated in
Fig. 3. These systems oscillate about the final value several
times, but with decreasing overshoot, until finally the
amplitude of the oscillations falls within the offset band.
Truly unstable systems, as illustrated in Fig. 4, oscil-
late about the final value indefinitely with increasing
amplitudes until one or more of the components in the
system saturates, at which time the amplitude of overshoot
remains constant.
Transient response to a step change.
  FIG. 3
Response of an unstable system.
  FIG. 4

There are mathematical ways to describe the degree of
stability, and these expressions are often used in in­dustry.
Stability in a linear feedback system exists only when
the exponents of the output equation are negative. If the
exponents were positive, the output would be as shown
in Fig. 4. Note that when we examined the ther­mometer
system above, the response equation was E = I (1 – Ae –t/
T1 + Be –t/T2); the exponents were de­termined solely by the
time constants.
Since the time constants for the real systems are, of
course, positive, this system will always have negative
exponents. (A positive exponent would require a nega-
tive value for Tl or T2 which is, of course, impossible.)
It is for this reason that all open loop systems are stable.
When feedback is employed, the exponents of the tran-
sient equations are no longer solely dependent on the
component time con­stants, but are also dependent on
the system gain. It is now possible for the interrelation
of time constants and gain to create positive exponents
in the transient equation, resulting in an unstable
system.
The amount or degree of damping of a system illus-
trated by Fig. 3 depends upon the magnitude of the damp­
ing ratio. When this ratio is less than one, the system
oscillates; when it is greater than one, the system is over-
damped, that is, its response is represented by Fig. 2.
Strictly speaking, the damping ratio should be called
a damping factor when it is equal to or greater than one.
For practical systems, a good value for the damping ratio
is about 0.6. Lower values cause over-sensitive systems that
produce excessive oscillations, while higher values cause
sluggish systems. Both of these systems require excessive
settling time.
Instability does not result from the undesirable prop-
erties of any one component alone, but rather from the
collection of properties, both good and bad, of all the com-
ponents. It is necessary, therefore, to realize that no matter
how insignificant component properties might seem, when
viewed alone, they may contribute a significant part to the
instability of the system.
When an actual control system is unstable, there are
a few standard cures; the first is usually to lower the
controller gain until the maximum permissible offset is
reached. If this is not sufficient to stabilize the system, the
gain is lowered still further until the system is stable and
The major points of interest in any control system are,
according to a decreasing order of importance:
1. System stability;
2. Satisfactory steady-state performance; and
3. Satisfactory response to transient conditions.
If a system is unstable, it is usually of no value whatso-
ever; therefore, the question of stability is of paramount
importance. If the steady-state performance is not sat-
isfactory, then adjustments or changes must be made
before transient conditions are checked.
Finally, if the system is stable and has satisfactory
steady-state performance, transient information is
required to determine the system response time and
overshoot.
It should be emphasized that it is absolutely essential
that we understand and can define the transient and
dy­namic characteristics of all the mechanical equipment
in an air-conditioning, refrigeration, heating or ventilating
system.
Unless these characteristics are known, we cannot
truly design or operate with optimum performance.
Fortunately, ASHRAE has embarked on a research program
called, Air Conditioning System Control, and the results
of this program will give us some indication of system
requirements.
It is imperative that more research of a similar nature
be carried out by ASHRAE until it becomes standard prac-
tice for manufacturers to publish the transient, as well as
the steady-state, characteristics of their equipment.
SUMMARY
then integral action is added to reduce the offset. Either
of these methods will work, but they certainly are not the
only methods that can be employed. It might also be pos-
sible to alter the properties of one or more components
in order to stabilize the system without increasing the
offset band and without resorting to reset action. Also, it
is true that in actual systems non-linearities affect stabil-
ity. Fortunately, however, some non-linearities are helpful
under certain conditions. Friction in a valve will help
dampen a highly oscillatory system.

Complex 3-D Airflow Networks
By Joseph W. Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., Fellow ASHRAE
L
ittle things can be big things. You build structures that
seem like they will work, and then the real world intrudes
and reminds you that you are not as smart as you think.
You realize that the fine points turn out to be vital.
It’s simple to build a compact unvented cathedral ceiling.
Take a flat unvented compact roof and slope it (Figure 1). No
big deal, right? Not quite. Sloping roofs can result in convection
cells, and worse. It turns out to be important to have multiple
layers of rigid insulation with the joints offset horizontally and
vertically (staggered) and to have an air barrier directly on the
structural deck (Figure 2). Ask me how I know?\*
Check out what this crazy person did: 8 in. (200 mm) of rigid
foam on the exterior walls and 10 in. (254 mm) of rigid foam in
one layer on the roof (Photo 1).† The key thing to note is that
the rigid foam on the roof is in one layer. Because it is in one
layer, it leads to convection (Photo 2).‡
The convection loop in this example is not catastrophic be-
cause it is not coupled to the interior space. Note the air barrier
sheet membrane under the rigid insulation. If the air barrier had
not been present, the convection cells would have been coupled
to the interior, and there could have been moisture transport
from the interior into the roof assembly. With moisture transport
from the interior, the roof likely would have been doomed as it
is located in a cold climate. As it is, the convection only lead
to professional embarrassment and an energy penalty. It turned
out that the professional embarrassment was much worse than
the energy penalty.
Let’s add a bit more complexity, and enjoy the outcome.
Structural insulated panels (SIPs) are an elegant enclosure
system: a foam core with exterior and interior linings of ori-
ented strand board (OSB). The panels slip together to make
walls and roofs and sometimes even floors and foundations.
Easy, fast, no thermal bridges, it can’t get better than that.
Let’s go to Juneau, Alaska, and check a bunch of SIP roof
assemblies (Photo 3). The first sign that something was amiss
were mushrooms growing out of the shingles. I call that the
Wolfgang Puck effect (Photo 4). Looking a little deeper (pun
intended), we find moisture damage along the panel joints and
the ridge line (Photo 5).
The pattern of damage is distinctive and can be found in
many cold climate SIP roof failures. It is called “ridge rot”
(Figure 3) and can easily be avoided by dealing with panel
joints. It is a classic case of three-dimensional airflow networks
and convection cells.
\* I have good judgment, and good judgment comes from experience, and experience comes from bad judgment. I have lots of experience.
† This is the world’s largest Canadian beer cooler under construction. It happens to be in Westford, Mass., and for years was my office before it became a bar. OK,
that’s not quite true. It has a bar inside one part of it. The rest of it is where building scientists hang out when they are not causing trouble. Note to the Standard 90.1
committee: see all that foam on the outside, which is something that you folks are saying can’t be done because it is not structurally possible, practical or safe?
Well, there it is. The cladding is 15 years old and does not appear to be falling off the building.
‡ When I first saw this after the first frost and I was standing on the sidewalk looking at my roof mumbling to myself that I am never, ever going to tell people about
just how stupid I am, this old guy walking his dog stops and looks up at the roof and then says to me: “You know that if you had put up that rigid insulation in two
layers with the joints offset horizontally and vertically, you would not have gotten those three-dimensional airflow networks.” I looked at him dumbfounded and asked,
rather humbled, who he was. He said: “I’m your neighbor, and I used to be a roofer.” Old guys know stuff. They can be a pain, but that’s because they know stuff.
Photo 1 (left): World’s largest Canadian beer cooler is located in Westford, Mass. Barn retrofit with 8 in. (203 mm) of rigid foam on the
exterior walls and 10 in. (254 mm) of rigid foam in one layer on the roof. Notice that the rigid foam on the roof is in one layer. Because it is
in one layer, it leads to convection. Photo 2 (right): Professional embarrassment. Convective cells evident in frost melt pattern on shingles.

about ASHRAE Journal, visit www.ashrae.org.
August 2009
ASHRAE Journal
65
The upper and lower horizontal SIP
splines have air gaps along their edges
creating parallel “micro-ducts” (Photo
6). The upper and lower pairs of micro-
ducts are connected at the panel joints
(Figure 4). If the panel joints are not
airtight at their interior surfaces, interior
moisture-laden air can migrate into the
interstitial joint cavities leading to the
pattern of damage observed.
Note that the damage is all along the
upper part of the roof—at the ridge—and
at the upper portions of the panel joints
near the ridge. The damage at the upper
portions of the roof assembly is due to
thermal buoyancy and moisture buoy-
ancy. The thermal buoyancy should be
obvious. We sometimes refer to it as the
stack effect. However, moisture buoyancy
is not always obvious. Yes, moisture-lad-
en air is less dense than dry air and tends
to rise. It is one of those counterintuitive,
but true things, which amaze people in
bars late at night.
How to avoid the problem of SIP
ridge rot? Make the panel joints airtight,
especially over structural supports such
as beams and purlins, and provide a
mechanism for moisture removal such
as a vented over-roof (Figure 5). It’s easy
once you know that it is important. Well,
we now know that it is important, and
SIPs can rock on in cold climates.
Let’s have some more fun and look at
what is becoming an increasingly com-
mon roof assembly failure with another
type of roofing panel. The intent is not to
pick on panels. Let’s go to Lexington, Ky.
Why, Lexington? It’s famous for bourbon,
of course, and because the example we
are going to discuss happens to be in
Lexington.
Photo 3 (left): SIP roofs. In Juneau, Alaska, rainfall is measured in feet not inches. Photo 4 (center): Mushrooms are growing out of the
roof. The Wolfgang Puck effect. Photo 5 (right): Moisture damage. Rot is located at panel joints and ridge.
Figure 1 (left): Slope an unvented compact roof. This should be easy. Figure 2 (right): Sloping unvented compact roof. It is important
to have an air barrier directly on the top of the structural roof deck and to have multiple layers of rigid insulation with the joints offset
horizontally and vertically. It is not a good idea to have only one layer of rigid insulation due to convection.

A sloping compact roof had water
running out of it during cold weather in
January and had water running out of it
during hot and humid weather in July.
There were never any reports of rainwater
leakage during or after rain. So, using
Sherlockian logic,§ this is clearly an air
transported moisture problem in need of
a building science explanation.
The roof assembly is a sloping fluted
metal deck (Photo 7) constructed with
vented foam panels with an OSB nail
base (Photo 8). The vented foam panels
are installed directly over the top of the
fluted metal deck. No air barrier is pres-
ent in the roof assembly and airflow can
easily be detected using an evaporative
thermal biometric airflow sensing device
(Photo 9). Rust stains are obvious at the
top surface of the fluted metal deck at
panel joints (Photo 10).
Once again, a complex three-dimen-
sional airflow network has rudely inter-
rupted the successful performance of
a sloping compact roof (Figure 6). The
situation is made worse because the fluted
metal roof deck is an “acoustical” deck
with perforations. When you are going to
screw up an assembly, you may as well
go all out.
The solution to this problem is ob-
vious. The roof assembly is in need
of an air barrier. There is no way that
the “nail-base panel” joints could
have been made airtight at the bottom
§Sherlock Holmes, of course. “Once you eliminate the impossible, whatever remains, no matter how improbable, must be the truth.” It can’t be rainwater because
there is no rain; it can’t be vapor diffusion because the underside of the panels (the OSB) and the panel foam cores are vapor barriers; so, the only transport mecha-
nism left that can move enough moisture to cause the observed damage is airflow. “Elementary, my dear Watson, elementary.”
Photo 6: Micro-air ducts. Note the air gap
on each side of the SIP joint spline creating
parallel micro-air ducts. The upper pair of
air ducts are typically connected to the lower
pair of air ducts at panel joints.
Figure 4: Airflow at joints. Upper and lower
splines are discontinuous, have gaps along
their edges and are connected at panel joints.

the panel joints airtight, especially over

better solution would have been to use staggered layers of
insulation over the top of the air barrier (Figure 7).
Air barriers, staggered layers of rigid insulation and sealed
panel joints at interior panel perimeters are the tools in the
building science toolbox that can be used to deal with complex
three-dimensional airflow networks and convection. Simple
little things that turn out to be pretty big things.
Joseph W. Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., is a principal of Build-

ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
A u g u s t 2 0 0 9
Energy-Efficient
Dehumidification
By John Dieckmann, Member ASHRAE; Kurtis McKenney,
and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
Dehumidification Basics
Air�cond�t�one�s�cool��ndoo��a���(sen-
conditioners�cool��ndoo��a���(sen-
cool indoor air (sen-
sible cooling) and dehumidify the air
(latent cooling). Conventional unitary
air conditioners typically have dehumi-
dification (latent) capacity in the range
of 20% – 30% of the total cooling ca-
pacity, or a sensible heat ratio (SHR) of
70% – 80%.
At a range of ambient conditions,
typified by high wet-bulb temperature
or high relative humidity with moderate
temperature, the latent cooling load will
be greater than 30% of the total cooling
load. This often leads to inadequate
dehumidification, requiring overcooling
of the conditioned space or use of hot
gas reheat, both inefficient practices, or
compromised indoor humidity control
and indoor air quality.
Furthermore, as building envelopes
are improved, the main benefit is re-
duced sensible load, with latent loads
becoming a larger proportion of the
total cooling load. Without cooling
equipment that can efficiently meet
latent load independently of sensible
loads, much of the potential energy
savings from improved building enve-
lopes will not be achieved in practice.
This issue is cited in the DOE Building
Technology Roadmap1, where item J
is, “Supplemental dehumidification
systems for humid climates—Deve-
lopment of efficient, reliable, low cost
supplemental dehumidification systems
for hot, humid climates that are capable
of maintaining internal RH below 50%
during periods when the demand for
sensible cooling is low.”
Dehumidification Approaches
In a basic vapor compression cycle
air conditioner, moisture is removed by
cooling the air to be dehumidified below
its dew point, causing moisture to con-
D
ehumidification load with partial sensible load occurs with an estimated
one-third of the primary energy consumption for air conditioning in the
Southern region of the U.S., a quarter of the primary cooling energy in the
Northeast region, and a quarter of the primary cooling energy in the Midwest
region. The Southern region accounts for more than 60% of the total U.S. cool-
ing energy consumption, while the Northeast and the Midwest regions account
for approximately 10% and 12%, respectively. Therefore, choosing the right
dehumidification approach is critical to saving energy.
dense out of the air, (Figure 1a). The air
is cooled by a refrigerated cooling coil
(the evaporator) and moisture condenses
on the fin surface of the coil and drains
off the coil by gravity into a condensate
pan and is sent to a drain. The amount
of dehumidification that is accomplished
relative to the sensible cooling is inher-
ently limited by the fact that it is neces-
sary to reduce the temperature of the air
to reach the point where moisture will
condense.
Figure 1b illustrates the simplest en-
hanced dehumidification approach, hot
gas reheat. With hot gas reheat, excess
sensible cooling is simply undone with a
heating coil downstream of the evapora-
tor. When high-pressure vapor from the
compressor discharge is the source of
heat for reheating (the “hot gas”), extra
energy is not used, but significant energy
is expended to accomplish the sensible
cooling that is undone. This is a highly
inefficient way to obtain dehumidifica-
tion with reduced or no sensible cooling.
Moreover, hot gas reheat results in little
or no increase in the dehumidification
capacity of the air conditioner.
The performance (both the efficiency
and the amount of moisture removed for
a given refrigerant compressor capacity)
can be improved by using the cold air
leaving the evaporator to precool the en-
tering air before it enters the evaporator.
This is done by recuperating, allowing the
evaporator and compressor to cool the air
to a lower temperature and remove more
moisture, as shown in Figure 1c.
With recuperative precooling and re-
heating, the overall effect is to exchange
sensible cooling capacity for additional
latent cooling capacity. In the Figure
1c configuration, a coil upstream and
a coil downstream of the evaporator
perform the recuperative precooling and
reheating. The heat that is removed from
the incoming air is transported by heat
pipes to the downstream coil, where it
is transferred to the cold dry air leaving
the evaporator. This arrangement, which
is produced commercially, can nearly
double the dehumidification capacity and
reduce the sensible capacity (reducing
Vapor Compression, Cromer Cycle
This article was published in ASHRAE Journal, August 2009. Copyright 2009 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning
Engineers, Inc. Posted at www.ashrae.org. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission
of ASHRAE. For more information about ASHRAE Journal, visit www.ashrae.org.
August 2009
ASHRAE Journal
79
the SHR to approximately 40%) without increasing compres-
sor power.
Alternatives to heat pipes include
using the refrigerant in the air-condi-
tioning cycle to transport heat between
the precooling and reheating coils, or
an air-to-air heat exchanger to transfer
heat from the incoming return air to
the cold air leaving the evaporator.
Optionally, for conditions requiring
little or no sensible cooling, additional
reheating can be provided by adding a
hot gas reheat coil.
The Cromer Cycle
Another alternative, the Cromer
Cycle, illustrated in Figure 2, com-
bines a desiccant wheel with a cool-
ing coil to provide augmented latent
heat capability.2,3 In equipment
that use the Cromer Cycle, building
return air passes through one side of a desiccant wheel, then
through the cooling coil, then through the other side of the
desiccant wheel. The desiccant wheel transfers moisture from
the cooled, saturated air exiting the evaporator to the return air,
while transferring sensible heat from the return air to the sup-
ply air. With the reduced temperature and increased humidity
in the return air entering the evaporator, the evaporator is able
to remove more moisture.
The Cromer Cycle produces similar enhanced dehumidifi-
cation performance as is obtained with recuperative configu-
rations. As shown in Figure 2, a preheat coil can be used to
preheat the return air, and by doing so, the sensible cooling
capacity can be reduced or eliminated, as needed. Cromer
Cycle equipment requires the additional cost of the desic-
cant wheel, a motor drive, a secondary condenser coil, and
associated controls.
Energy Savings Potential
Air-conditioning systems with
enhanced, energy-efficient dehu-
midification are applicable to all
regions of the U.S. where latent
cooling requirements frequently
exceed 20%–30% of the total
cooling load. Or, in terms of cost
effectiveness, energy-efficient de-
humidification is applicable when
the energy savings resulting from
the enhanced dehumidification
capability exceeds the capital
cost of the additional equipment
required. Unfortunately, there is
a dearth of information regarding
the latent cooling load in U.S.
that is not accompanied by a full
sensible cooling load. Consequently, a rough estimate of the
potential energy savings impact has been made, based on the
assumption that dehumidification load with partial sensible
load occurs with one third of the primary energy consumption
for air conditioning in the South region of the U.S., a quarter
of the primary cooling energy in the Northeast region, and a
quarter of the primary cooling energy in the Midwest region.
Note also that the South region accounts for over 60% of the
total U.S. cooling energy, while the Northeast and the Midwest
regions account for approximately 10% and 12%, respectively.
According to the 2008 Buildings Energy Data Book,4 in 2006
the primary energy consumed for space cooling of residential
and commercial buildings was 4.9 quads, predominately asso-
ciated with electricity consumption. Based on our assumptions,

References
1. U.S. Department of Energy, Energy Efficiency and Renewable
Energy. 2008. Building Technologies Program, Planned Program
Activities for 2008 – 2012. pp. 2 – 7, and Table 2 – 6, “Residential Inte-
gration Technical Challenges/Barriers.” http://tinyurl.com/DOE2008.
2. ACEEE. 2004. “Cromer Cycle air conditioners,” Emerging Tech-
nologies & Practices. http://tinyurl.com/aceee2004.
3. Cromer, C. 2001. “Cromer Cycle air conditioner: a study to
confirm target performance.” Florida Solar Energy Center. http://
tinyurl.com/cromer01.

John Dieckmann is a director and Kurtis McKenney is a
senior technologist with TIAX, Cambridge, Mass. James Bro-
drick, Ph.D., is a project manager with the Building Technolo-
gies Program, U.S. Department of Energy, Washington, D.C.
one-quarter of the total energy consumption in the U.S. occurs
when enhanced latent cooling is needed.
Both recuperative and Cromer Cycle cooling can approxi-
mately double the dehumidification capacity and latent cooling
efficiency of conventional air-conditioning equipment, meaning
that there is an energy savings potential of approximately half
a quad if air-conditioning systems with recuperative dehumi-
dification were universally adopted in the assumed regions of
applicability. Beyond considerations of energy conservation,
better indoor humidity control is directly correlated with a
healthier indoor environment and the avoidance of other high-
humidity related problems such as mold growth.
Market Factors
Generally, equipment that enables enhanced dehumidi-
fication capability in an energy-efficient fashion will have
additional first cost. Whether heat pipes or additional heat
exchanger volume for recuperative dehumidification, or
desiccant wheels and drives for Cromer Cycle systems, the
additional first costs need to be justified by energy cost sav-
ings, or the less quantifiable comfort benefits. Furthermore,
designers, owners, and contractors need to understand which
